title: AC TO DC CONVERSION
abstract: A method of AC-to-DC conversion is disclosed, comprising steps of: rectifying an AC voltage to a pulsating DC voltage; coupling the pulsating DC voltage to a capacitor via a switch; coupling an output voltage of the capacitor to a load; monitoring a signal of the load; determining a voltage deviation of the signal of the load from a predetermined reference; in synchronization and in every cycle of the pulsating DC voltage, turning on the switch at a first time instant when the switch is not forward biased and turning off the switch in response to the voltage deviation at a second time instant; whereby the signal of the load is controlled.
claims:
Claims CLAIMS The invention claimed is: 1. A method of AC-to-DC conversion, comprising the steps of: rectifying an AC voltage to a pulsating DC voltage; coupling the pulsating DC voltage to a capacitor via a switch; coupling an output voltage of the capacitor to a load; monitoring a signal of the load; determining a voltage deviation of the signal of the load from a predetermined reference; in synchronization to and in every cycle of the pulsating DC voltage, turning on the switch at a first time instant when the switch is not forward biased and turning off the switch in response to the voltage deviation at a second time instant; whereby the signal of the load is controlled. 2. The method of claim 1 , wherein the load signal is representative of a load voltage, or a load current, or a load power. 3. The method of claim 1 , further comprising the step of synchronization through detecting zero-crossing of a voltage across the switch. 4. The method of claim 1 , further comprising the step of synchronization through detecting zero-crossing of the AC voltage. 5. The method of claim 4, wherein zero-crossing detection is comprising the steps of: monitoring a voltage at each of the two input terminals of the AC voltage; comparing the voltages to a predetermined threshold value close to zero; determining when both voltages are below the threshold value. 6. The method of claim 4, wherein zero-crossing detection is comprising the steps of: determining the sum of the voltages at the two input terminals of the AC voltage; comparing the sum to a predetermined threshold value close to zero; determining when the sum is below the threshold value. 7. The method of claim 1 , further comprising the step of determining an on-time for the switch, wherein the on-time being inversely proportional to the voltage deviation. 8. The method of claim 7, wherein the step of determining the on-time is further comprising the steps of: generation of a sawtooth signal in synchronization to the AC voltage; comparing the sawtooth signal with the deviation of the signal of the load; whereby a signal of pulse width equal to the on-time is generated. 9. The method of claim 1 , further comprising the step of comparing the deviation of the signal of the load to the pulsating DC voltage, wherein the switch is turned off when the pulsating DC voltage is higher than the deviation. The method of claim 1 , further comprising the step of converting the output voltage of the capacitor to a target voltage through a voltage regulator, wherein the output voltage is controlled to be no less than the target voltage plus an optimal threshold voltage. 1 1 . The method of claim 10, further comprising the steps of: determining a peak-to- valley ripple amplitude of the output voltage of the capacitor; determining a threshold voltage which is the sum of the target voltage, a preassigned voltage-drop across the voltage regulator and the peak-to-valley ripple amplitude; comparing the threshold voltage with the pulsating DC voltage; and turning the switch off when the pulsating DC voltage is higher than the threshold voltage. The method of claim 10, further comprising the steps of: determining a valley value of the output voltage of the capacitor, determining an error signal being a sum of the target voltage and a preassigned voltage-drop across the voltage regulator minus the valley value; amplifying the error signal; comparing the amplified error signal with the pulsating DC voltage; and turning the switch off when the pulsating DC voltage is higher than the amplified error signal. 13. The method of claim 10, wherein the voltage regulator is a linear regulator. 

 An AC-to-DC converter, comprising: a rectifying means to convert an AC voltage to a pulsating DC voltage; a capacitor coupled to the pulsating DC voltage vi a switch; a load coupled to an output voltage of the capacitor; means for synchronization to the pulsating DC voltage; means for determining a deviation of a load signal from a predetermined reference; wherein in synchronization to and in every cycle of the pulsating DC voltage, turning on the switch at a first time instant when the switch is not forward biased and turning off the switch in response to the deviation at a second time instant; whereby the signal of the load is controlled. 15. An AC-to-DC converter of claim 14, wherein synchronization is through a zero crossing detector of a voltage across the switch. 16. An AC-to-DC converter of claim 14, wherein synchronization is through a zero crossing detector of the AC voltage. 17. An AC-to-DC converter of claim 16, wherein the zero-crossing detector is further comprising: means for detecting an input voltage at each of the two input terminals of the AC voltage; a comparator to compare each of the input voltages to a predetermined threshold value close to zero; and a logic AND gate to determine if both voltages are below the threshold value. An AC-to-DC converter of claim 16, wherein the zero-crossing detector is further comprising: a means for summation of the input voltages at the two input terminals of the AC voltage; a means to compare the sum to a predetermined threshold value close to zero; whereby zero-crossing is detected if the sum is below the threshold value. 19. An AC-to-DC converter of claim 14, further comprising a means for determining an on-time for the switch, wherein the on-time being inversely proportional to the voltage deviation. 20. An AC-to-DC converter of claim 19, wherein the means for determining the on- time for the switch is further comprising: a sawtooth signal generator synchronized to the AC voltage; a first comparator to determine a deviation of the signal of the load from a predetermined reference, a second comparator to compare the sawtooth signal with the deviation; whereby a signal of pulse width equal to the on-time is generated. 21 . An AC-to-DC converter of claim 14, further comprising: a means to amplify the deviation of the signal of the load from the reference; a comparator to compare the deviation with the pulsating DC voltage; wherein the switch is turned off when the pulsating DC voltage is higher than the deviation. 22. An AC-to-DC converter of claim 14, further comprising a voltage regulator to convert the output voltage of the capacitor to a target voltage; wherein the output voltage is controlled to be no less than the target voltage plus an optimal threshold voltage. 23. An AC-to-DC converter of claim 22, further comprising: a means to detect a peak-to-valley ripple amplitude of the output voltage of the capacitor; means for determining a threshold as a sum of the target voltage, a preassigned voltage-drop across the voltage regulator and the peak-to-valley ripple amplitude; means to compare the threshold with the pulsating DC voltage; wherein the switch is turned off when the pulsating DC voltage is higher than the sum. 24. An AC-to-DC converter of claim 22, further comprising: a means for determining a valley value of the output voltage of the capacitor, means for determining an error signal as a sum of the target voltage and a preassigned voltage- drop across the voltage regulator minus the valley value; means for amplifying the error signal; means for comparing the amplified error signal with the pulsating DC voltage; wherein the switch is turned off when the pulsating DC voltage is higher than the amplified error signal.
